extra.message: User input from request.POST.get('name') and request.POST.get('pass') is concatenated directly into an SQL string passed to login.objects.raw(). An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL by supplying crafted values for 'name' or 'pass', bypassing authentication or exfiltrating data.
User input from request.POST.get('name') and request.POST.get('pass') is concatenated directly into an SQL string passed to sql_lab_table.objects.raw(). This allows SQL injection attacks.

User-controlled input 'domain' (from request.POST.get('domain')) is formatted into a shell command string ('nslookup {}' or 'dig {}') and executed with shell=True via subprocess.Popen. Although a URL-prefix regex is stripped, the domain value is not sanitized against shell metacharacters (e.g. ';', '|', '&&'), allowing OS command injection.
User-controlled input from request.POST.get('val') is passed directly to Python's built-in eval(). This allows arbitrary code execution on the server.

cwe: CWE-95: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code

extra.message: The 'token' cookie value is base64-decoded and passed to pickle.loads() without any integrity verification or signature check. An attacker who can craft or modify the cookie can achieve arbitrary code execution via a malicious pickle payload.

The SAX parser is created with feature_external_ges set to True and the raw request body is parsed directly with parseString(). This enables XML External Entity (XXE) attacks: an attacker can read arbitrary files from the server or trigger SSRF via external entity references in the submitted XML.

cwe: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

extra.message: User-supplied POST parameter 'blog' is joined with the view's directory via os.path.join() and then opened with open(). There is no path sanitization or restriction, allowing an attacker to supply '../../../etc/passwd' or similar paths to read arbitrary files on the server (path traversal).

User-supplied POST parameter 'url' is passed directly to requests.get() with no validation or allowlist. An attacker can force the server to make HTTP requests to arbitrary internal or external destinations (SSRF), potentially reaching internal services or cloud metadata endpoints.

User-supplied blog content from request.POST['blog'] is passed through filter_blog() (which does nothing — it returns the input unchanged) and then written verbatim into a Django template file on disk. The template is subsequently rendered via render(). This allows Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI): an attacker can inject Django template directives (e.g., {% debug %}, custom tags, or filter chains) to exfiltrate settings, secrets, or execute code.

User-uploaded file content is passed to yaml.load() with yaml.Loader (the full Loader). yaml.Loader supports arbitrary Python object construction via !!python/object tags and is known to allow arbitrary code execution. yaml.safe_load() should be used instead.

User-controlled input from request.POST.get('function') is passed to Pillow's ImageMath.eval(). In Pillow versions before 10.3.0, ImageMath.eval() is backed by Python's eval() and allows arbitrary code execution. An attacker can supply '__import__("os").system("id")' or similar expressions.

cwe: CWE-95: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code

extra.message: User-controlled input 'ip' from request.POST.get('ip') is concatenated directly into the shell command 'nmap ' + ip and executed with shell=True. An attacker can inject shell metacharacters (e.g. '; cat /etc/passwd') to execute arbitrary OS commands.

User-controlled POST parameter 'expression' is passed directly to Python's built-in eval(). This allows arbitrary server-side code execution. The endpoint also lacks @authentication_decorator (commented out at line 213), meaning unauthenticated users can exploit it.

cwe: CWE-95: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code

extra.message: User-supplied form data 'serialized_data' is base64-decoded and passed to pickle.loads() with no integrity verification. An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload to achieve arbitrary code execution on the server.

The unsanitized GET parameter 'q' is passed to the template context as 'query' and rendered in xss_lab.html. If the template uses {{ query|safe }} or similar, it will reflect unsanitized user input as HTML, enabling reflected XSS.
The XSS filter in xss_lab2 only removes literal '' strings from user input but does not sanitize other XSS vectors (e.g., '', '

xss_lab3 removes only alphanumeric characters from POST input 'username' using re.sub, explicitly allowing special characters like []()!+ for JSFuck-style payloads. The result is rendered in xss_lab_3.html as 'code', enabling XSS via JSFuck or other non-alphanumeric JavaScript encoding.

The 'userid' session cookie is set with secure=False and samesite=None. It is not HttpOnly either (httponly parameter not set, Django defaults vary by version). This exposes the session cookie to JavaScript (XSS theft) and cross-site requests.

cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute

extra.message: auth_lab_login authenticates users solely based on the 'userid' cookie value, which is an auto-incrementing integer. Any user can impersonate any other user by forging or guessing the userid cookie value, with no cryptographic verification.
ba_lab grants admin access by checking if the client-controlled cookie 'admin' equals '1'. An attacker can set this cookie in their browser to gain admin privileges without valid credentials.
a1_broken_access_lab_1 grants admin access by checking if the client-controlled cookie 'admin' equals '1'. An attacker can set this cookie to bypass access controls.

a1_broken_access_lab3_secret only checks if the user is authenticated (is_authenticated), but does not verify if the user has admin role. Any authenticated user can access the secret page, demonstrating broken access control / missing authorization.

User password is hashed using MD5 (hashlib.md5) before database comparison. MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password hashing. It is vulnerable to preimage and collision attacks and has precomputed rainbow tables available.

cwe: CWE-916: Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort

extra.message: The crypto_failure_lab3 view stores an unencrypted and unsigned cookie of the form 'username|expiry_datetime'. An attacker can trivially forge the cookie (e.g., 'admin|<future_date>') to gain admin access, as there is no HMAC or signature verification.

cwe: CWE-345: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

extra.message: The JWT token for the CSRF lab is signed with the hardcoded weak secret 'csrf_vulneribility'. An attacker who knows this secret can forge JWT tokens and impersonate any user.

csrf_transfer_monei_api performs financial transfers (balance deductions and additions) via a GET request with parameters in the URL path. Using GET for state-changing operations violates REST semantics and makes the endpoint trivially exploitable via CSRF (e.g., an attacker embeds an tag referencing the transfer URL). The @csrf_exempt decorator is also applied to the parent csrf_transfer_monei view.

The Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in settings.py as a static string. This key is used for cryptographic signing of cookies, sessions, and CSRF tokens. Exposure of this key allows an attacker to forge sessions and bypass CSRF protections.

DEBUG = True is set in settings.py. In debug mode, Django renders full tracebacks including local variables, settings values, and source code snippets on error pages, leaking sensitive information to any user who triggers an exception.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

extra.message: SECRET_COOKIE_KEY is hardcoded as 'PYGOAT' in settings.py and is used to sign JWT auth cookies in sec_misconfig_lab3. This trivially weak secret allows attackers to forge JWT tokens and gain admin access.

Flask secret key is hardcoded ('your-secret-key-here') and user credentials including plaintext passwords ('admin123', 'password123') are hardcoded in the source code. These can be read by anyone with access to the source.

Session tokens are generated by base64-encoding 'username:datetime.now()' with no HMAC or signature. An attacker can forge any session token by encoding an arbitrary username. The dashboard (line 111) also validates the session by simply base64-decoding the cookie and extracting the username.
Flask app is started with debug=True. This enables the Werkzeug interactive debugger which allows arbitrary Python code execution from the browser when an exception occurs.
all_users_data_view returns the credit card numbers, SSNs, and API keys of ALL users to any caller including unauthenticated users. There is no authentication or authorization check on this endpoint.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as 'django-insecure-key-for-demonstration-only' in the sensitive_data_lab settings. ALLOWED_HOSTS is also set to ['*'], accepting requests from any host, which can facilitate Host header injection attacks.
DEBUG = True is set in the sensitive_data_lab Django settings. This causes Django to expose full tracebacks and sensitive configuration details to users on error.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

extra.message: The ssrf_lab function joins a caller-supplied 'file' argument with the module's directory using os.path.join() and opens it for reading with no path restriction. This function is invoked with user-controlled test bench inputs in apis.py (ssrf_code_checker), enabling path traversal to read arbitrary files.

The OTP is a 3-digit number (randint(100, 999)) — only 900 possible values. For non-admin emails, the OTP is returned directly in the HTML response (line 506), completely exposing it. For admin@pygoat.com, there is no rate limiting or lockout, so the 900-value OTP space can be brute-forced trivially.

cwe: CWE-307: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts

extra.message: log_function_checker writes user-supplied POST parameters 'log_code' and 'api_code' directly to Python source files on disk (playground/A9/main.py and playground/A9/api.py) with no sanitization. These files are subsequently imported and executed as Python modules. This allows arbitrary code injection into the application.

A6_disscussion_api_2 writes user-supplied POST parameter 'code' directly to playground/A6/utility.py with no sanitization. This file defines the check_vuln function used by the application. An attacker can overwrite this file with arbitrary Python code that will be executed on the next invocation.

The 'secret' view reflects the user-supplied X-Host header value into the response without sanitization: 'Your X-Host is ' + XHost. This enables reflected XSS if the response is served as HTML, and also constitutes HTTP header injection if XHost contains newline characters.
Username 'jack' and password 'jacktheripper' are hardcoded in the view logic at a1_broken_access_lab_1. Anyone with access to source code knows valid credentials.
Credentials 'John'/'reaper' and 'admin'/'admin_pass' are hardcoded in a1_broken_access_lab_3 view logic.
